title: The Enemy by Pearl S. Buck
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Conflict between human compassion and national loyalty, duty as a doctor versus duty as a citizen, and humanity rising above hatred.

summary:
The story is set during World War II and revolves around Dr. Sadao Hoki, a Japanese surgeon trained in America, and his wife Hana. One night, they find an American soldier washed up on the beach near their house. He is a wounded prisoner of war with a gunshot wound and is bleeding heavily. Though Sadao knows that hiding an enemy is treason and punishable by death in Japan, his duty as a doctor compels him to save the man. He brings the soldier home, and he and Hana secretly operate on him. They remove the bullet and nurse him back to health.

The servants in their house are horrified and leave, calling Sadao a traitor. Even Hana is conflicted, torn between loyalty to her country and basic human compassion. Sadao also struggles. He is patriotic and knows the soldier is the enemy, yet he cannot let a man die when he has the skill to save him.

To resolve the conflict, Sadao informs the old General he is treating. The General promises to send private assassins to kill the soldier quietly. But the General forgets because he is self-centered and cares mainly about his own health. Realizing the danger, Sadao decides to help the soldier escape. He gives him a boat, food, water, clothes, and instructions to wait for a Korean fishing boat. He tells him to signal with a flashlight if he runs out of food. After the soldier leaves, Sadao waits each night but sees no light. He understands that the man has escaped safely.

qa.q: Who was Dr Sadao? Where was his house?
qa.a: Dr Sadao Hoki was a famous Japanese surgeon and scientist. He had studied surgery and medicine in America. His house was on the Japanese coast, built on rocks above a narrow beach.

qa.q: Will Dr Sadao be arrested on the charge of harbouring an enemy?
qa.a: No, Dr Sadao was not arrested. He told the General about the American prisoner. The General secretly planned to send assassins to kill the prisoner, but he forgot because of his own illness and operation.

qa.q: Will Hana help the wounded man and wash him herself?
qa.a: Yes, Hana helped the wounded man. When the maid Yumi refused to wash the white man, Hana washed him herself. Though she was afraid and disliked helping an enemy, her humanity made her help him.

qa.q: What will Dr Sadao and his wife do with the man?
qa.a: Dr Sadao and Hana first saved the wounded American soldier. Sadao operated on him and removed the bullet. Later, when the man recovered, Sadao helped him escape safely by giving him a boat, food, clothes, and a flashlight.

qa.q: Will Dr Sadao be arrested on the charge of harbouring an enemy?
qa.a: No, he was not arrested. The General did not punish him because he needed Dr Sadao as his doctor and had forgotten to send the assassins due to his illness.

qa.q: There are moments in life when we have to make hard choices between our roles as private individuals and as citizens with a sense of national loyalty. Discuss with reference to the story you have just read.
qa.a: In the story, Dr Sadao faces a hard choice. As a Japanese citizen during war, he should have handed over the American prisoner to the authorities. But as a doctor and human being, he could not let a wounded man die. He chose humanity and professional duty over narrow national hatred.

qa.q: Dr Sadao was compelled by his duty as a doctor to help the enemy soldier. What made Hana, his wife, sympathetic to him in the face of open defiance from the domestic staff?
qa.a: Hana became sympathetic because she saw the prisoner as a suffering human being. During the operation, she noticed his pain and scars and wondered whether prisoners were tortured. Her compassion and respect for her husband’s duty made her help him despite the servants’ opposition.

qa.q: How would you explain the reluctance of the soldier to leave the shelter of the doctor’s home even when he knew he couldn’t stay there without risk to the doctor and himself?
qa.a: The soldier was weak, wounded, and afraid. Dr Sadao’s house was the only safe place he had found. Outside, he could be captured, tortured, or killed. So he naturally felt reluctant to leave the shelter.

qa.q: What explains the attitude of the General in the matter of the enemy soldier? Was it human consideration, lack of national loyalty, dereliction of duty or simply self-absorption?
qa.a: The General’s attitude was mainly self-absorption. He did not care about national duty or the prisoner. He cared only about his own health and needed Dr Sadao for his operation. He forgot about sending assassins because he was focused on himself.

qa.q: While hatred against a member of the enemy race is justifiable, especially during wartime, what makes a human being rise above narrow prejudices?
qa.a: Human compassion, professional duty, moral courage, and the ability to see another person as a human being make people rise above narrow prejudices. Dr Sadao saves the American soldier because his duty as a doctor and his humanity are stronger than hatred.

qa.q: Do you think the doctor’s final solution to the problem was the best possible one in the circumstances?
qa.a: Yes, Dr Sadao’s final solution was the best possible one. He saved the soldier’s life and then helped him escape. This protected the soldier, reduced danger to his own family, and avoided direct betrayal of his country in a public way.

qa.q: Does the story remind you of ‘Birth’ by A. J. Cronin that you read in Snapshots last year? What are the similarities?
qa.a: Yes. Both stories show doctors performing their duty with dedication. In Birth, the doctor saves a mother and child; in The Enemy, Dr Sadao saves a wounded enemy soldier. Both stories show the importance of medical duty and human life.

qa.q: Is there any film you have seen or novel you have read with a similar theme?
qa.a: A similar theme can be seen in stories or films where people help enemies during war because humanity is greater than hatred. Such stories show that compassion can survive even in times of violence and conflict.
